---
title: "Quiz: Hidden Markov Models"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

Short questions to test your understanding of HMMs: the four ingredients, the
generative story, the path-probability formula, and the three classic problems.
This is the hardest day of the block, so make sure the vocabulary is solid. Try
each question before opening the hint or answer.

Throughout, use the Day-9 model: states $\{H,L\}$ (H = GC-rich, L = normal),
symbols $\{A,C,G,T\}$,
$$
\boldsymbol{\pi}=\begin{bmatrix}0.5\\0.5\end{bmatrix},\quad
A=\begin{bmatrix}0.8&0.2\\0.3&0.7\end{bmatrix}\ (HL\times HL),\quad
B=\begin{bmatrix}0.1&0.4&0.4&0.1\\0.3&0.2&0.2&0.3\end{bmatrix}\ (ACGT).
$$

---

**Q1.** In a CpG-island HMM, which layer do you **observe** and which is
**hidden**: the DNA bases, or the "GC-rich vs normal" region labels?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
You can read the sequence off a sequencer. Nobody hands you the island
annotation.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
You **observe** the **DNA bases** (the emissions). The **region labels**
(GC-rich vs normal) are the **hidden states** you must infer.
:::

---

**Q2.** Name the four ingredients of an HMM and say which of them are *new*
compared with a visible Markov chain (Day 7).

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
The hidden layer is just a Day-7 chain. What extra object connects hidden states
to observations?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
(1) hidden **states**, (2) **transition matrix** $A$, (3) **emission matrix**
$B$, (4) **initial distribution** $\boldsymbol{\pi}$. The transition matrix and
initial distribution are the same objects as a visible Markov chain; the genuinely
**new** ingredient is the **emission matrix $B$** (and the fact that the states
are now hidden).
:::

---

**Q3.** Both $A$ and $B$ have rows that sum to 1, but they are different shapes.
What are the dimensions of each, and what does a row of each represent?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
$A$ goes state → state; $B$ goes state → symbol.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
$A$ is **states × states** ($2\times2$ here); a row is the distribution over
the **next state**. $B$ is **states × symbols** ($2\times4$ here); a row is the
distribution over the **emitted symbol** for that state.
:::

---

**Q4.** Write the generative story (the order of steps) for producing a length-2
sequence from an HMM.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Start, emit, move, emit. You always finish on an emission.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
(1) Draw $z_1$ from $\boldsymbol{\pi}$. (2) Emit $x_1$ from row $z_1$ of $B$.
(3) Draw $z_2$ from row $z_1$ of $A$. (4) Emit $x_2$ from row $z_2$ of $B$.
:::

---

**Q5.** Using the model above, compute $P(\mathbf{x}=(C),\ \mathbf{z}=(H))$ for
a single position.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Length 1: one start factor, one emission factor, no transitions.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
$P = \pi_H \cdot B_{H,C} = 0.5 \times 0.4 = 0.20$.
:::

---

**Q6.** For $\mathbf{x}=(G,G)$ and path $\mathbf{z}=(H,H)$, compute the joint
probability.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
$\pi_H \cdot B_{H,G} \cdot A_{H,H} \cdot B_{H,G}$.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
$0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.8 \times 0.4 = 0.064$.
:::

---

**Q7.** For a sequence of length $T$, how many $\boldsymbol{\pi}$, emission, and
transition factors appear in a single path probability $P(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})$?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
One start; one emission per position; one transition per gap between positions.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**1** initial factor, **$T$** emission factors, and **$T-1$** transition
factors.
:::

---

**Q8.** What is the difference between the **probability of one path**
$P(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})$ and the **likelihood of the data** $P(\mathbf{x})$?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
One fixes a path; the other accounts for every path that could have produced
$\mathbf{x}$.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
$P(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})$ is for **one specific** hidden path. The likelihood
$P(\mathbf{x})=\sum_{\mathbf{z}}P(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})$ **sums over all**
possible hidden paths.
:::

---

**Q9.** Match each classic HMM problem to its algorithm: likelihood, decoding,
learning.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Forward, Viterbi, Baum–Welch — one each.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
Likelihood → **forward** algorithm; decoding (most likely path) → **Viterbi**;
learning the parameters from unlabelled data → **Baum–Welch** (EM).
:::

---

**Q10.** Which of the three problems produces the **predicted CpG-island
annotation** of a genome, and how do you read the islands off its output?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Which algorithm returns one hidden-state label per position?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**Decoding** (Viterbi). It returns the most likely hidden state at each
position; the **maximal runs of the $H$ (GC-rich) state** are the predicted CpG
islands.
:::

---

**Q11.** Why can't you compute the likelihood of a whole chromosome by simply
listing all hidden paths and summing, the way you did for length 2 by hand?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
How does the number of paths grow with sequence length?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
The number of paths is **exponential** in the sequence length ($S^T$ for $S$
states, length $T$), so for millions of positions it is astronomically large.
The **forward algorithm** computes the same sum efficiently by reusing partial
results instead of enumerating paths.
:::

---

**Q12.** Baum–Welch is said to find only a **local** optimum. What practical
consequence does this have when you fit an HMM to real sequence?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Think about how the starting parameter guess affects where it ends up.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
The fitted parameters depend on the **initial guess**, so different starts can
give different results. In practice you try several initialisations, sanity-check
that the learned states are biologically interpretable, and do not blindly trust
a single run.
:::
